categoryDescription: Looks at the ETF's risk profile across five lenses: risk-adjusted return (with worst-drawdown, capture, and stress-window-recovery context), risk handling vs the fund's category peers, macro environment risk (economy, industry cycle, interest rates, currency), the structural risk built into this group of fund, and stress liquidity / exit-friction (premium-discount blowout, bid-ask blowout, AP roster health). Pass/Fail is always mandate-based: a high-beta thematic fund is expected to swing; a low-vol fund is expected to be calm; a covered-call fund is expected to trade upside for downside protection; a managed-futures fund is expected to decorrelate from equities — each is judged against its own job, not against a broad-equity benchmark. `factorDescription` carries the generic measurement principle and the Pass/Fail rule. `groupInstructions` carries the group-specific perspective (typical Sharpe / vol range, comparison index, drawdown norm, peer guardrails, the macro forces that matter for that group, the structural risk that applies, and the stress-liquidity behavior of representative peer funds). When the two seem to conflict, follow `groupInstructions`.

factors.factorTitle: Are You Paid Fairly for the Risk
factors.factorDescription: Measures how much return investors earned per unit of risk taken, using Sharpe (excess return ÷ total volatility) and Sortino (excess return ÷ downside volatility). The pass bar is category-specific. This factor also folds in the downside-protection check: in real stress windows (2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock, 2008 GFC where applicable), did the fund's drawdown match what its Sharpe / strategy promised? A fund explicitly marketed for downside protection — low-volatility, buffer / defined-outcome, market-neutral, managed-futures / systematic trend, downside-hedge, allocation, target-date — with a respectable Sharpe but near-100% downside-capture is failing the practical risk-adjusted test. NOTE: dividend / yield / quality / value tilts are NOT defensive-sold products — they are equity exposure with a screen, not downside-protection products; do not apply the defensive-sold Fail to them. For active funds, Sharpe is the honest test of whether manager picks added real risk-adjusted value; for passive funds, Sharpe vs category tells you whether the index itself was efficient. For funds younger than 3 years, Sharpe is unreliable and the short history must be stated. Pass when Sharpe is at or above category median over the longest available multi-year window, Sortino is consistent with Sharpe (no hidden downside story), and the stress-window drawdown matches what the mandate promised. Fail when Sharpe materially trails category median without a mandate reason, OR Sortino is materially weaker than Sharpe, OR a defensive-sold fund (as defined above) showed no meaningful drawdown protection in the latest stress.
factors.factorMetrics: sharpe, sortino, portfolioRiskScore, morAnalyzerRiskReturn, captureRatios, drawdown, riskVsCategory, returnVsCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Sharpe above 0.5 is decent and above 1.0 is very good over a multi-year window. Compare to the S&P 500's Sharpe in the same window — a passive Large Blend should be within tracking distance; an active or tilt fund should show whether the tilt paid for the extra risk it took. Drawdown ratio close to 1.0 vs the index is expected for passive — that's the asset class, not a Fail. Verdict band: Strong = return-per-risk ≥2 pp better than category; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse without a mandate-aligned reason.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector Sharpe is more volatile than broad-equity Sharpe — read multi-year context (tech and discretionary lead in growth cycles, defensives in late-cycle). Do not Fail a sector fund for a wrong-half-of-cycle Sharpe; the honest test is whether Sharpe is at or above the sector-peer median over a multi-year window. Stress drawdown vs the sector index, not the S&P 500. Verdict band: Strong = ≥2 pp better than sector-peer median; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Multi-year Sharpe is essentially meaningless here — daily-reset decay destroys the risk/return relationship. Judge on short-horizon suitability and on whether realized returns track the leverage multiple of the underlying with reasonable fidelity, not on the long-window Sharpe number. Drawdown is mechanically the leverage factor times the underlying's drawdown minus reset slippage — quote both numbers side by side. Verdict band does not apply here — do NOT impose a pp band on these funds.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Sharpe varies a lot by window (BTC has swung from very high to deeply negative inside two years; gold ranges 0.2 to 0.8 over multi-year windows). Read multi-year context and judge against the asset's own history, not against equity Sharpe. Honest drawdown test: gold should show positive or near-flat returns in equity drawdowns (the diversification claim) — a gold fund that fell with equities in 2022 has lost its hedging case. Verdict band: Strong = ≥2 pp better than wrapper-peer median; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Derivative-income and hedge-style funds can show low or even negative Sharpe during equity bull markets yet still deliver on a hedging mandate — judge against category peers, not against broad equity. Drawdown test is the honest one: covered-call funds should show meaningfully lower drawdown than the underlying (JEPI -13% vs S&P -25% in 2022 = mandate met). Market-neutral and managed-futures should be roughly flat or positive in equity bear years; if not, mandate is not being delivered. Verdict band: Strong = ≥2 pp better than peer-category median (within the right sub-bucket); In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Sharpe of 0.5-1.0 is typical for allocation and target-date funds — a moderate-allocation fund matching 60/40 returns with lower vol is doing its job even if absolute return lags pure equity. Compare to a passive 60/40 same-period Sharpe AND check the drawdown ratio vs 100% equity in 2022 / 2020 (moderate should land at roughly 60-70% of S&P's drawdown). Verdict band: Strong = ≥2 pp better than allocation-peer median; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Sharpe is structurally lower (0.2-0.5 normal) because both excess return and volatility are compressed. For passive Treasury / IG funds, Sharpe vs category tells you whether the index itself was an efficient exposure — do not Fail a passive fund for matching its index's Sharpe. Drawdown in 2022 should match the duration-matched Treasury index. Narrow verdict band for bonds: Strong = ≥0.5 pp better than category; In Line = within ±0.5 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥0.5 pp worse.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Sharpe is usually 0.3-0.6 mid-cycle and can go deeply negative in credit shock years (2008, 2020 partial). Multi-year Sharpe matters more than single-year; compare to the matching credit benchmark's Sharpe in the same window. Drawdown test: HY drew -22% in 2008, -15-20% in 2020 — in-line with the credit index is Pass; materially worse suggests excess exposure to lower-rated or concentrated issuers. Narrow verdict band for credit: Strong = ≥0.5 pp better than the credit-tier peer median; In Line = within ±0.5 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥0.5 pp worse.

factors.factorTitle: How This Fund Handles Risk vs Its Category Peers
factors.factorDescription: How does this fund's risk profile compare to its same-category peers across 3Y / 5Y / 10Y? Use category-relative risk score, peer-relative risk level, and the four-outcome test: above-average risk WITH above-average return = acceptable trade; above-average risk WITHOUT above-average return = clear Fail; below-average risk with similar-or-better return = strong risk discipline; below-average risk with weaker return = trading return for safety (fine for conservative sleeves). Always report peer-group size alongside the rank — median in a 600-fund category is different from median in a 10-fund category. For passive funds inside an active-heavy peer category, structural fee + tracking-cost headwind means median-vs-active is a Pass-grade outcome. Pass when risk is at or below category median, OR the extra risk is clearly compensated by better category-relative returns, OR the fund is a passive tracking its index inside an active-heavy peer set. Fail when risk sits consistently above the category median across multiple periods without better returns to justify it.
factors.factorMetrics: riskScore, riskLevel, riskVsCategory, returnVsCategory, riskPeriods, percentileRanks, quartileRanks, numberOfInvestmentsInCategory, overviewCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Risk-to-category should track the index for passive funds; do not Fail a passive Large Blend for category-like risk — that is the index moving. For style tilts (value, growth, low-vol, momentum, quality), check whether the tilt's risk profile matches its claim — a 'low-volatility' fund whose category risk reads at or above median is failing its mandate. Verdict band: Strong = return-vs-category ≥2 pp better; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse on the return side without a risk discount that justifies it.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector categories are tight (Technology, Health, Real Estate, Utilities, Communications); thematic and miscellaneous categories more dispersed (Miscellaneous Sector, Theme, Equity Digital Assets). Single-country EM categories have small peer counts (India Equity, China Region, Latin America Stock) — report the count alongside the rank. Compare drawdown depth in sector down-cycles to sector peers. Verdict band: Strong = ≥2 pp better than sector-peer median on returnVsCategory; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Risk-vs-category is mostly daily-tracking quality across the leveraged peer set — does this 3x track better than the other 3x in the category? Structural decay applies to every product in the category, so do not Fail on rank alone if the decay is in line with peers. Watch for tracking divergence in stressed underlyings (the inverse oil and inverse-volatility blowups of 2018 / 2020). Verdict band does not apply here — judge on tracking quality, not on a pp band.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Small peer counts (Commodities Broad Basket, Commodities Precious Metals, Digital Assets, Single Currency) — report peer count alongside any rank. Physical-backed vs futures-based wrappers in the same category have different risk profiles (futures-based carry roll-cost drag); note which sub-group the rank is against. For crypto, custody structure and issuer track record matter alongside the headline risk metrics. Verdict band: Strong = ≥2 pp better than wrapper-peer median on returnVsCategory; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Derivative Income, Defined Outcome, Equity Hedged, Multistrategy, Systematic Trend, Long-Short Equity, Market Neutral, Macro Trading, Multialternative, Event Driven, Relative Value Arbitrage, Volatility, Downside Hedge are separate categories with different risk norms. Within Derivative Income especially, dispersion is wide (JEPI vs QYLD vs SPYI have different option mechanics, underlying indices, and ROC composition). Compare to the right sub-category. Verdict band: Strong = ≥2 pp better than the right sub-category peer median; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare within the specific dated vintage (Target-Date 2030, 2040, 2050…) or allocation bucket (Conservative / Moderate / Aggressive / Tactical / Global Allocation / Target Risk / Target Outcome). Risk vs category should reflect the stated bucket; a 'moderate' fund whose risk sits with the 'aggressive' peer group is mis-bucketed and should be flagged. Verdict band: Strong = ≥2 pp better than the right vintage / bucket peer median; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp worse.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Duration-bucketed peer comparison (Ultrashort, Short-Term, Intermediate Core, Long-Term, Long Government, Inflation-Protected). Stay inside the exact category. For muni, compare within national vs single-state buckets. Narrow verdict band for bonds: Strong = ≥0.5 pp better than category; In Line = within ±0.5 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥0.5 pp worse.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Credit-tier peer comparison (High Yield Bond, Bank Loan, Preferred Stock, Convertibles, Emerging Markets Bond, Multisector Bond, Nontraditional Bond, High Yield Muni, Securitized Bond-Focused). Compare within the specific category. Narrow verdict band for credit: Strong = ≥0.5 pp better than the credit-tier peer median; In Line = within ±0.5 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥0.5 pp worse.

factors.factorTitle: Macro Risk — Economy, Industry Cycle, Rates, Currency
factors.factorDescription: How sensitive is the fund to macro forces — broad economic cycles, sector / industry cycles, interest-rate path, currency moves, and global macro shocks? Different funds carry different macro exposures: equity funds carry economic-cycle risk; bond funds carry interest-rate risk magnified by duration; international funds add currency risk; sector funds carry industry-cycle risk; commodity funds carry commodity-cycle and geopolitical risk; crypto funds carry regulatory and adoption-cycle risk; allocation funds inherit the macro risk of their underlying sleeves. The retail question: 'What macro environments would hurt this fund, and how badly?' Use the fund's behavior in past macro shocks (2008 GFC, 2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock, 2018 trade war, 2014-2016 oil crash) as the empirical test. For young funds without that history, judge by stated exposures and category analogues. Pass when macro sensitivity is consistent with mandate and category — a long-duration bond fund that lost 25-30% in 2022 was doing what duration does; a global allocation fund that gave up 5pp to USD strength in 2022 was bearing currency risk inherent to the mandate. Fail when macro exposure is materially larger than the category norm without being disclosed, OR when the fund makes an unannounced macro bet (large duration, large country tilt, large sector concentration) that retail holders cannot easily see.
factors.factorMetrics: duration, beta, beta1y, beta2y, beta5y, riskAndVolatilityMeasures, riskPeriods, returnsAnnual

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Economic-cycle risk is the dominant macro factor — recessions drop broad equity -20% to -35%. Foreign and global funds add currency risk: a USD-strengthening year like 2022 cost foreign-equity returns to USD investors materially. Fed-cycle matters too — growth-tilted funds suffer more in rising-rate cycles than value-tilted; high-dividend funds behave like a duration substitute when rates fall.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Industry-cycle risk is the primary macro factor — tech sensitive to rates and capex cycles; energy to oil prices and OPEC+ decisions; financials to the yield curve; REITs to rates; biotech to FDA and reimbursement policy; utilities and consumer staples are defensive but rate-sensitive. Single-country EM adds currency, political, and capital-controls risk (China's tech regulatory crackdown 2021-22, India's rupee moves, Latin American currency shocks).
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Macro shocks are amplified by the leverage factor. A 3x long-equity fund in a Fed-tightening cycle is a leveraged bet that no recession lands soon; a 3x long-bond fund is a leveraged duration bet. State the macro position retail is implicitly taking. Inverse funds in trending environments compound favorably; in choppy environments they bleed regardless of direction.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Commodity-cycle risk (oil supply / OPEC+, agricultural seasonality, mining production, industrial demand); USD strength inversely correlates with commodity prices; geopolitical events drive sharp moves (Middle East, Russia / Ukraine, OPEC+ decisions, sanctions). Crypto carries regulatory risk (SEC enforcement actions, country-level bans, stablecoin rules) and adoption-cycle risk; post-2022 BTC has correlated with risk-on / risk-off equities more than its 'digital gold' marketing suggests.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Sensitive to the volatility regime — option premium is small in low-vol regimes (covered-call yield shrinks), large in high-vol regimes. Long-short and managed-futures depend on trend / dispersion environments; market-neutral suffers in crowded-quant unwinds. Buffer / defined-outcome funds carry interest-rate risk through their option-pricing and reference-rate components. Macro stress test: 2008 GFC, 2018 vol spike, 2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock — how did each strategy hold up?
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Macro risk is the blend of equity, bond, and (sometimes) alt sleeves. Rising-rate environments hurt the bond sleeve materially — 2022 cost moderate-allocation funds -16% even though equity-only was -25%, because the typical 40% bond sleeve lost ~-13%. Currency exposure for global allocation funds adds another layer. Tactical funds carry manager-call risk on top of asset-class risk.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Interest-rate risk is the single dominant macro: duration × rate move = expected price loss. Long-duration government / muni (15Y+) lost -25% to -31% in 2022; intermediate core (5-7Y) -10% to -15%; ultrashort (<3Y) a few percent; MMF essentially flat. Match the fund's duration to the investor's holding horizon and rate view; long-duration is a directional rate bet. Foreign IG adds currency considerations (hedged vs unhedged matters materially).
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Credit-cycle risk is the primary macro — recessions widen spreads and trigger downgrades / defaults (2008 GFC: HY -22%; 2020 COVID: HY -15-20%, bank loans -5-10%). Rate sensitivity is secondary for longer-dated members (preferred 5-6Y duration, EM debt 6-8Y, convertibles often closer to equity). EM debt layers currency, sovereign, and geopolitical risk. Bank loans and AAA CLOs are partly insulated from rates (floating-rate coupons reset).

factors.factorDescription: Some ETF groups carry a structural risk built into how the fund is run — separate from market moves (those live in `macro_environment_risk` and `risk_adjusted_return`) and separate from how the fund behaves in stress windows (that lives in `stress_liquidity_and_exit_friction`). Common mechanics by group: daily-reset compounding decay in leveraged / inverse products; return-of-capital eroding NAV in covered-call wrappers and some preferred / convertible / EM-debt funds; contango / roll cost in futures-based commodity wrappers; single-name and sub-sector concentration plus closure risk in narrow sector / thematic funds; glide-path drift in target-date funds; yield-smoothing or credit drift in some bond wrappers. Try to find the most relevant structural mechanic for this fund's group. If no clear group-specific mechanic applies AND the related risks are already covered by other factors in this report (drawdown, risk-adjusted return, macro, stress liquidity), mark Pass — do NOT invent a risk just to fill the factor. Where a mechanic does apply, the factor also asks: is the strategy delivering enough return / income / utility to justify the structural cost? Pass when no group-specific mechanic meaningfully applies, OR the mechanic exists but the strategy is paying for it. Fail only when the mechanic is clearly present AND is hurting retail returns without offsetting value.
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return1y, return3y, return5y, return10y, change1y, change5y, change10y, returnsAnnual, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, overviewTtmYield, dividendTtm, divGrowth3y, divGrowth5y, beta, beta1y, drawdown, aum

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Broad-equity funds rarely carry a unique structural mechanic — fee drag belongs to the cost report, and beta / drawdown / macro live in the other risk factors. Look for one of: an active manager quietly drifting from the stated mandate, a benchmark change in recent years, or a tracking gap on a passive fund that is materially wider than the expense ratio. If none of those apply, mark Pass — do not force a structural-risk read here.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Two structural risks. (1) Concentration risk — top-10 weight (below ~40% diversified, 40-60% typical, above 60% means fund fate is tied to a handful of names); single-name max (anything above 10% is meaningful single-stock risk — several semiconductor funds carry ~15% NVDA); sub-sector / single-country concentration inside a theme. (2) Thematic-fund liquidation risk — when AUM falls below the survival threshold, the issuer may close or merge the fund and retail holders are forced out at a bad time. Cross-check the AUM trend. Fail when concentration is unusually high AND not disclosed by the marketing label, OR AUM is below the closure threshold.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Structural NAV erosion from daily-reset path dependency is the central risk. Quote the underlying's CAGR × the stated leverage factor as the textbook expectation, show the realized result — the gap IS the decay. Strategy test: leveraged products are useful only for short-term directional trading. Pass when daily-tracking quality is tight and the product is correctly marketed as short-term. Fail when daily tracking has broken down, OR the product is being marketed for buy-and-hold.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Two sub-types. Futures-based wrappers (USO, UNG, broad-commodity futures) carry contango / roll-cost drag — USO lost ~94% of NAV since inception. Quote the spot-vs-fund gap. Physical-backed gold / silver and spot-crypto wrappers (GLD, IAU, IBIT, FBTC) do not have this mechanic; their structural risk is custody (insurance, audit, issuer track record). Name which sub-type the fund belongs to. Pass when the strategy justifies the drag (diversification or directional exposure) or there is no drag. Fail when futures-roll cost has eaten multi-year price returns without delivering the diversification it claimed.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Return-of-capital propping distributions is the central structural risk. Break out the ROC share of the latest year (often 40%+ for QYLD-style funds, lower for JEPI). Show the 5Y / 10Y NAV path alongside cumulative distributions — a flat-to-positive total return on top of a steadily declining NAV is 'paying you with your own money'. Strategy test: covered-call funds should deliver yield + capped upside + cushion in down markets. Pass when all three are present and ROC is moderate (under ~30%). Fail when ROC dominates AND the underlying long-term price has materially declined; QYLD vs JEPI is the textbook contrast.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Structural risks here are about how the fund is built, not market moves. Three to check: (1) Glide-path design — target-date funds are either 'to-retirement' (equity drops to a final low at the dated year and stays there) or 'through-retirement' (equity keeps drawing down for years past the dated year, which can deepen drawdowns in retirement). Name which kind this fund uses. (2) Bond-stock correlation breakdown — a 60/40 lost roughly 16% in 2022 because bonds and stocks fell together; the diversification cushion the mandate promises is not always there. (3) Sleeve complexity — many target-date funds hold 20-40 underlying funds with mixed passive / active sleeves; flag if the all-in cost is materially higher than peers. Pass when the glide-path design is clearly disclosed, the fund has tracked its published curve, and distributions come from natural sleeve yield. Fail when the glide path has drifted, the design isn't clearly labeled, or distributions materially exceed the natural sleeve yield.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Structural risks here are about how the fund mechanically reports income and holds bonds, not market moves (those are in `macro_environment_risk`). Three to check: (1) Yield smoothing — when TTM yield is materially higher than SEC yield, the fund is paying de-accumulated coupons or smoothing distributions; the headline yield will eventually fall. (2) Credit-quality drift — a 'core IG' fund holding 30%+ BBB or non-IG splinters is reaching for yield outside its mandate. (3) Tax mechanics retail underestimates — TIPS funds generate phantom income (inflation accruals are taxable annually even though uncollected as cash); muni funds may carry AMT exposure or lose state-tax exemption for out-of-state holders. Pass when SEC and TTM yields are close, credit mix matches the marketed band, and any tax quirks are disclosed. Fail when TTM materially exceeds SEC, credit mix has drifted, or a structural tax quirk would surprise retail given the marketing label.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Structural risks vary by sub-type. Four to check: (1) Return-of-capital in distributions — preferred, convertible, and some EM-debt wrappers can carry a material ROC share that silently lowers cost basis; break it out where present. (2) Capital-stack position — CLO funds hold tranches (AAA vs mezzanine vs equity) with very different stress behavior; preferred-stock funds sit below all bondholders and can be skipped on dividends in stress. (3) Liquidity-in-stress — bank loans, CLOs, and EM-debt sub-types can trade at deep discounts or face gating in panics; a real friction for retail wanting to sell. (4) Reaching-for-yield drift — credit-tier mix should match the marketed bucket. Strategy test: if HY underperformed IG over a 5Y / 10Y window, the credit risk was not paid for. Pass when ROC is modest, capital-stack position matches the marketing, liquidity is acceptable, and credit mix is on-mandate. Fail when any of those fail materially.

factors.factorDescription: Asks what happens to the fund's tradability when markets dislocate — separate from normal-market trading cost. Three stress behaviors matter: (1) premium/discount blowout — the gap between market price and NAV widens when authorized-participant arbitrage breaks down, so retail selling pays a meaningful haircut on top of the price drop (March 2020 saw HY corporate ETFs trade at 5%+ discounts to NAV for days; muni ETFs and EM-debt ETFs dislocated similarly). (2) Bid-ask spread blowout — normal-market 5 bps can become 50-200 bps in stress windows, exactly when retail is most likely to sell. (3) AP roster thinness and underlying-basket liquidity — funds with few active APs or illiquid underliers are most exposed. This is a tail-event risk, not a daily-cost question — the cost question lives in the fee report. Asset-class-wide vs fund-specific: when the whole peer category dislocated similarly in the same stress window (e.g. every HY ETF traded at a 4-6% discount in March 2020), that is structural to the wrapper and the underlying market, NOT a fund-specific failure — Pass with clear disclosure of the asset-class behavior so retail understands the wrapper. Fail only when this fund dislocated materially MORE than its peers in the same window. Pass when the fund has a broad AP roster AND liquid underliers AND a track record of disciplined premium/discount behavior in past stress windows, OR when any past dislocation was asset-class-wide and the fund tracked peers. Fail when the fund dislocated materially worse than its peers in past stress events, OR underliers are structurally illiquid (frontier markets, bank loans, deep HY) AND the fund lacks the offsetting AP and AUM scale that its peers have.
factors.factorMetrics: overviewTotalAssets, marketVolumeAvg, avgVolume, dollarVol, marketBidAskSpread, marketDiscount, marketPremium, premiumDiscountHistory, navHistory, riskPeriods, drawdownDates, overviewCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Major broad-equity ETFs (VOO, VTI, IVV, SPY) hold up well in stress — tight spreads, premiums/discounts within a few bps even on bad days. Smaller broad-equity ETFs from second-tier issuers can see spread widening. International broad-equity carries timezone-based dislocation (the fund trades while underlying markets are closed) — note this as a structural feature.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector ETFs (XL- series) stay disciplined. Thematic ETFs with illiquid underliers (single-country EM, micro-cap thematic, frontier sectors) can dislocate 50-200 bps in stress. Smaller thematic funds with <$50M AUM are most exposed.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Major leveraged products (TQQQ, SOXL, UPRO) trade tightly even in extreme volatility because of massive volume. Smaller leveraged products — especially on single stocks or thinly-traded indices — have shown bid-ask blowouts and tracking failures in stress (the inverse-volatility products in February 2018 are the canonical case).
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Physical-backed gold ETFs (GLD, IAU) and major spot crypto ETFs (IBIT, FBTC) trade tightly. Futures-based commodity funds can dislocate when futures markets gap. The pre-conversion Grayscale GBTC trust traded at 30-40% discount to NAV for years — flag any wrapper that lacks proper AP creation/redemption.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: JEPI, JEPQ, QYLD trade tightly in normal markets. Smaller derivative-income funds and defined-outcome products can dislocate in vol spikes. The options-based machinery is also exposed to dealer-pricing breakdowns in extreme moves.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Major target-date and allocation ETFs trade tightly. Tactical-allocation ETFs holding less-liquid components can dislocate. Allocation mutual funds (not ETFs) face the separate end-of-day NAV-redemption risk that retail in taxable accounts should know about.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Treasury ETFs (IEF, TLT, VGIT) trade tightly even in stress because the underlying is the most liquid market on earth. Core IG (AGG, BND) holds up well. Muni ETFs can dislocate 20-50 bps in stress because munis are OTC; single-state muni dislocations can be deeper.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Most exposed group. HYG, JNK, LQD traded at 5%+ discounts in March 2020 before AP arbitrage caught up. EM debt (EMB), bank loans (BKLN), and preferred ETFs (PFF) all show meaningful stress dislocation. This is structural, not a flag against the specific fund — but retail needs to know that 'I can sell whenever' is only true in calm markets.
